Metadata.homeTitle: Precisar · Empower how you use technology
Metadata.homeDescription: Empower how you use technology. Home with hero, rotating verbs, ticker, and stacked sections. Precisar 2026.

homeConvoca.intro: Every day, more information is produced and circulated than we can process.

chile.stat2Label: of the citizenry does not understand what they read
chile.outro: In Chile, access is no longer the only gap. The challenge today is to strengthen people’s ability to understand, analyze, and usefully apply the information that runs through their everyday and civic lives—not only in the news sphere or solely in digital settings, but also in services, institutions, platforms, and other spaces where people need to find their bearings and take action.

propuesta.body1: Information arrives in fragments. AI answers before you finish your question. Media change faster than we can make sense of them. And yet, everyone already navigates all of that—in their own way, with what they have.
propuesta.body2: At Precisar we strengthen that capacity: so more people can interpret the information they receive and build their own meaning.

homePrograms.title: Programs
homePrograms.stackHeadline: Millions are connected. With judgment, many are still missing.
homePrograms.body: We design tools, content, and training experiences—in person and online—that strengthen critical thinking, digital security, and civic engagement.
homePrograms.stackLinkCiudades: Cities Connected with Purpose — Workshops and support for municipalities to strengthen digital skills across the community.
homePrograms.stackLinkHub: Hub Digital Consciente — Immersive digital culture experiences for all ages, adaptable to any space.
homePrograms.stackLinkAprender: Learn Digital: It's Never Too Late — A programme for older adults who want to connect with more safety and autonomy.
homePrograms.stackLinkPensamientoCritico: Media education for teachers — Tools and resources to help teachers bring critical thinking into the classroom.
homePrograms.stackLinkFuncionarios: Course for public servants — Practical digital skills training for people who work in government.
homePrograms.stackBotOndaManoDesc: For messages, chains, iffy links, and voice notes. It helps you read signals, understand what’s reliable, and use AI with judgment.
homePrograms.stackBotOndaCivitaDesc: For making sense of the public sphere: the state, laws, the economy, the environment, and recent history. Clear language, context, and data.
homePrograms.stackBotOndaProfesDesc: For teachers and education teams: activities, good practice, rubrics, and transparent use of AI in the classroom.
homePrograms.stackBotOndaEscuchaDesc: Onda doesn’t just answer. It can also gather questions, experiences, and real signals from people who use the chat, so the team can review them and better understand what’s missing from the explanations.

ciudades.title: Cities Connected with Purpose
ciudades.body: We run workshops and support institutions and municipalities in designing and implementing media and digital literacy strategies. Through participatory diagnostics and community workshops, residents gain skills to access reliable information, develop critical thinking, and participate confidently in the digital environment.

hub.title: Hub Digital Consciente
hub.body: Immersive digital culture experiences for all ages, adaptable to events, municipalities, and educational settings. Through posters, videos, and user-centred interactive applications, we explore topics such as AI, disinformation, and privacy—turning them into participatory experiences that encourage reflection on technology’s impact on society.

aprender.title: Learn Digital: It’s Never Too Late
aprender.body: A programme designed especially for older adults, with in-person and online workshops that combine practical device use, digital safety, and social activities—strengthening their autonomy and connection with family and community.

homeParticipa.stackHeadline: How do you get informed today?
homeParticipa.stackTagline: Your experience changes how all of us get informed.
homeParticipa.stackBody: We want to understand how you receive information today and how you would like to receive it: which formats help you, which tone builds trust, and what you need to understand better, access more easily, and share with more judgment. Your answers go straight to media outlets, institutions, and decision-makers so they can improve how they inform the public.

homeEducacionMediatica.stackHeadline: Understanding information is also a skill you learn.
homeEducacionMediatica.stackBody:
Four dimensions for reading the digital world
with more judgment, more awareness, and more autonomy.

homePrecisando.stackHeadline: How information circulates—and what effects it produces.
homePrecisando.stackBody: Precisando is Precisar’s editorial: articles and debate to read the digital environment with more context, judgment, and depth. Explore recent posts and categories.
homePrecisando.stackUnderConstruction: Under construction. Articles will be published here soon.

homeSaberes.stackHeadline: Knowing isn’t hoarding data. It’s knowing what to do with it.
homeSaberes.stackBody: Three collections of open resources—to reflect and broaden perspectives.

homeBotOnda.stackHeadline: Onda doesn’t just answer. It listens, too.
homeBotOnda.stackBody:
Onda en Mano, Onda Civita, and Onda Profes: three chat spaces to guide you without replacing your judgment.
Service in beta.

culturaDigitalPage.metaDescription: Thirty-six perspectives on algorithms, privacy, disinformation (information integrity), digital citizenship, and conscious online practices.

comunicacion.metaDescription: Communication axis — media literacy: media ecosystem, freedom of expression, mass media, disinformation (information integrity), and social networks.
comunicacion.heroIntro: This axis is an invitation to think of communication not only as something we receive, but also as something we create, share, and transform.
paras: Communication is the essential starting point to understand how our ideas about the world are built. It’s not just about traditional media, but about the entire ecosystem that produces, distributes, and reshapes the information we consume daily—from a news bulletin to a meme on social networks.

This axis proposes looking closely at the path information travels, questioning who produces it, why, and with what intention. It also invites us to strengthen the right to express ourselves, to inform ourselves freely, and to recognise when that right is at risk—through censorship, disinformation (information integrity), or the invisibilisation of certain communities.
comunicacion.leadRest: it can connect or divide, give visibility or exclude. That’s why this axis promotes a critical and active citizenry, capable of:
listItems: Identifying manipulation mechanisms (such as fake news).
Taking part in their own communication experiences, such as community media.
Defending freedom of expression with responsibility.
Stepping out of bubbles and polarisation to listen to other perspectives.

cultura.metaDescription: Culture axis — media literacy: cultural diversity, media representation, digital culture, participation, dialogue, and the right to communicate.
cultura.heroIntro: Culture is identity, memory, and creation. Media literacy lets us inhabit that space with awareness and a voice of our own.
paras: Culture is not an ornament: it’s the way we live together, recognise each other, and express ourselves. It’s in what we consume, how we speak, in the memes we share, in the stories we inherit and in those we decide to transform.

In the digital environment, culture multiplies and changes rapidly. Youth narratives, viral content, expressions from native peoples, women’s activism, digital aesthetics, and public debates on networks are as much part of the cultural field as a book, a festival, or a work of art.

Media literacy enters here not to judge but to expand. It gives us tools to understand which meanings are reproduced, which are made invisible, and how we can intervene in creative, respectful, and transformative ways.

A cultural approach to media literacy recognises the right to communicate from diversity, fosters intergenerational dialogue, and promotes a citizenry able to build its own narratives, beyond hegemonic discourses.

educacion.metaDescription: Education axis — media literacy: critical thinking, classroom, production, information assessment, and digital life.
educacion.heroIntro: This axis seeks to transform the relationship between citizenry and media from the root: critical thinking, lifelong learning, and the capacity to act with awareness in the public sphere.
educacion.subhead: Learning throughout life to live in a critical, conscious, and active digital environment
paras: This axis focuses on building media competencies from childhood through older adulthood. It’s not just about teaching how to use technology, but about shaping a citizenry able to understand, analyse, and take part in building the digital and informational world.

Media literacy, in this sense, is a tool for autonomy and participation that should be present throughout life: in childhood, as a basis for critical reading; in adolescence, to strengthen digital identity; in adulthood, to defend against disinformation (information integrity) and make informed decisions; and in old age, to ensure inclusion, bonds, and rights.
listItems: Integrating media and information literacy into school curricula, not as an isolated subject but as a cross-cutting competence.
Supporting teachers and educators with contextualised tools, methodologies, and content.
Creative and reflective content production (videos, podcasts, memes, articles) as a way of learning by doing.
Developing critical thinking, informational reading, and skills to navigate digital environments.
Promoting training spaces for adults and older adults, recognising their knowledge and specific needs.
educacion.closing: Media literacy is not just for students or experts. It’s a right for every person, at any stage of life, and a condition for building more democratic, informed, and empathetic societies.

tecnologia.metaDescription: Technology axis — media literacy: algorithms, privacy, digital divide, AI, and conscious use of platforms.
tecnologia.heroIntro: This axis addresses how technology shapes the way we inform ourselves, communicate, and take part in digital life. It’s not just about using devices, but about understanding how they influence what we see, think, and decide.
tecnologia.openBold: Media literacy in a technological key is not only about learning to use tools, but about understanding the systems that govern them.
paras: We live in a hyper-connected society, where devices, digital platforms, and algorithms mediate not only the information we receive, but also our emotions, decisions, and ways of relating. In this scenario, the Technology axis invites us to look beyond the interface.

Here, media literacy becomes a practice of autonomy. Because those who don’t understand how technology operates are exposed to invisible manipulations: algorithm logic, personal data collection, viralisation of hate, or the creation of information bubbles.

This axis does not seek to teach people to use technology “correctly,” but to inhabit it with awareness, question it with critical thinking, and transform it with ethics. It means shaping citizens who can protect their privacy, spot biases in automated systems, detect AI-generated content, and demand a fairer and more transparent digital environment.
tecnologia.closing: Technology and media intersect in everyday life. Understanding that intersection is key to digital citizenship.

comunicacion.title: Communication
comunicacion.description: Precisar media literacy axis: how information is produced and circulates, and how to exercise the right to inform ourselves and to speak with judgment.
paras: This axis is an invitation to think of communication not only as something we receive, but also as something we create, share, and transform.

Communication is the essential starting point to understand how our ideas about the world are built. It’s not just about traditional media, but about the entire ecosystem that produces, distributes, and reshapes the information we consume daily—from a news bulletin to a meme on social media.

This axis proposes to look closely at the path information travels, to question who produces it, why, and with what intention. It also invites us to strengthen the right to express ourselves, to inform ourselves freely, and to recognise when that right is at risk—through censorship, disinformation (information integrity), or the invisibilisation of certain communities.

Communication is not neutral: it can connect or divide, give visibility or exclude. That’s why this axis promotes a critical and active citizenship, capable of identifying manipulation mechanisms (such as fake news), demanding transparency from the media, taking part in their own communication experiences, defending free speech with responsibility, and stepping out of bubbles and polarisation to listen to other perspectives.
comunicacion.listTitle: Keys to understand the media world
listItems: Freedom of expression and right to information
comunicacion.closing: It’s the set of media, platforms, and actors that produce and disseminate information. It ranges from large channels to social networks and community media. Understanding who communicates, how, and with what interests is key to reading better what we see.

educacion.title: Education
educacion.description: Media literacy throughout life: skills to read the digital world with autonomy and participation, from Precisar.
paras: This axis seeks to transform the relationship between citizenry and media from the root: critical thinking, lifelong learning, and the capacity to act with awareness in the public sphere.
Learning throughout life to live in a critical, conscious, and active digital environment.

This axis focuses on building media competencies from childhood through older adulthood. It’s not only about teaching how to use technology, but about shaping a citizenry able to understand, analyse, and take part in building the digital and informational world.

Media literacy, in this sense, is a tool for autonomy and participation that should be present throughout life: in childhood, as a basis for critical reading; in adolescence, to strengthen digital identity; in adulthood, to defend against disinformation (information integrity) and make informed decisions; and in old age, to ensure inclusion, bonds, and rights.
actions: Integrating media and information literacy into school curricula as a cross-cutting competence.
Supporting teachers and educators with contextualised tools.
Creative and reflective content production as a way of learning by doing.
Developing critical thinking, informational reading, and skills to navigate digital environments.
Promoting training spaces for adults and older adults, recognising their knowledge and specific needs.
educacion.afterActions: Media literacy is not just for students or experts. It’s a right for every person, at any stage of life, and a condition for building more democratic, informed, and empathetic societies.
educacion.listTitle: Keys to learning with critical sense
educacion.closing: It’s not just about having opinions, but about knowing why we have them. Critical thinking is the ability to observe, interpret, and question the world around us, including the media messages we consume.

cultura.title: Culture
cultura.description: Identity, memory, and creation in the digital environment: media literacy to inhabit culture with one’s own voice, with Precisar.
paras: Culture is identity, memory, and creation. Media literacy allows us to inhabit that space with awareness and a voice of our own.

Culture is not an ornament: it’s the way we live together, recognise each other, and express ourselves. It’s in what we consume, how we speak, in the memes we share, in the stories we inherit and in those we decide to transform.

In the digital environment, culture multiplies and changes quickly. Youth narratives, viral content, expressions from native peoples, women’s activism, digital aesthetics, and public debates on networks are as much part of the cultural field as a book, a festival, or a work of art.

Media literacy enters here not to judge but to expand. It gives us tools to understand which meanings are reproduced, which are made invisible, and how we can intervene in creative, respectful, and transformative ways.

A cultural approach to media literacy recognises the right to communicate from diversity, fosters intergenerational dialogue, and promotes a citizenry able to build its own narratives, beyond hegemonic discourses.
cultura.listTitle: Keys to understand culture in media literacy
listItems: To create is to take part
cultura.closing: Culture is not one, homogeneous, or static. It’s multiple, changing, and alive. Media literacy invites us to recognise that diversity as a value, not an obstacle.

tecnologia.title: Technology
tecnologia.description: How technology and algorithms shape what we see and decide: Precisar media literacy axis for digital autonomy.
paras: This axis addresses how technology shapes how we inform ourselves, communicate, and take part in digital life. It’s not just about using devices, but about understanding how they influence what we see, think, and decide.
Media literacy in a technological key is not only about learning to use tools, but about understanding the systems that govern them.

We live in a hyper-connected society, where devices, digital platforms, and algorithms mediate not only the information we receive, but also our emotions, decisions, and ways of relating. In this scenario, the Technology axis invites us to look beyond the interface.

Here, media literacy becomes a practice of autonomy. Because those who don’t understand how technology operates are exposed to invisible manipulations: algorithm logic, personal data collection, viralisation of hate, or the creation of information bubbles.

This axis does not seek to teach people to use technology “correctly,” but to inhabit it with awareness, question it with critical thinking, and transform it with ethics. It means shaping citizens who can protect their privacy, spot biases in automated systems, detect AI-generated content, and demand a fairer and more transparent digital environment.
Technology and media intersect in everyday life. Understanding that intersection is key to digital citizenship.
tecnologia.listTitle: Keys to understand technology in media literacy
tecnologia.closing: Technology is not neutral. What we see on social networks, search engines, or platforms is mediated by algorithms that prioritise some content and silence other. Understanding how these systems work is key to developing a critical view of the information we consume.

participaPage.metaTitle: How do you get informed today?
participaPage.title: How do you get informed today?
participaPage.lead: Help us understand what information is useful to you, what worries you, and which formats really work in your day-to-day.
participaPage.consultaBody: Share your opinion in the external form and check the associated privacy policy.
participaPage.writeTeamBuilding: This form is under construction. Write directly to contacto@precisar.net
participaPage.writeTeamRedirectBefore: On submit, we will redirect you to

somos.metaDescription: We strengthen capacities to understand, assess, and use information with more judgment and meaning. Co-design with municipalities, education, civil society, and public services in Chile.
somos.heroTitle: We strengthen capacities to understand, assess, and use information with more judgment and meaning
intro: At Precisar we work with organisations that want to strengthen people’s relationship with information and promote a more conscious, useful, and constructive use of technology.

We work with municipalities, educational communities, civil society organisations, public services, media outlets, and territorial networks to translate a complex problem — the unequal capacity to understand, assess, and use information — into concrete tools, methodologies, and resources adapted to real contexts.
somos.principle: We don’t work from closed solutions. We start from context, listen to how people experience this problem, and design with each partner ways to address it with practical sense, clear language, and capacity to implement.

problem.title: What problem we address
paras: We understand information in a broad sense. It refers not only to news or journalistic content, but also to institutional, civic, digital or non-digital, educational, and practical information that people receive, interpret, and use to solve problems, take part in public matters, access rights, and operate in digital environments.

Today the issue is no longer only connectivity. Although connectivity has advanced, a deep gap remains in people’s actual capacity to understand, assess, and use information usefully in everyday and civic life.

That gap is not limited to news or social media. It also includes institutional, educational, practical, and service-related information: the kind people need to orient themselves, solve problems, interact with institutions, and make informed decisions.

When that capacity is missing, confusion grows, distinguishing reliable information becomes harder, communication between institutions and citizens weakens, and barriers to participating, understanding, and acting with judgment widen.

items.title: We co-design with context
items.body: We don’t apply one-size-fits-all formulas. We adapt methodologies, tools, and resources to the needs, languages, and capacities of each organisation and the communities they work with.

items.title: We translate evidence into action
items.body: We produce and organise useful evidence to understand how people get informed, what barriers they face, and which tools can be more effective in supporting them.

items.title: We test in the field
items.body: We design and implement experiences in real contexts, with concrete audiences and diverse conditions, to learn, adjust, and leave replicable models.

items.title: We transfer capacities
items.body: We aim for organisations to take ownership of methodologies, resources, and tools, integrating them into their own practices beyond a one-off intervention.

items.title: We combine clarity, technology, and public purpose
items.body: We develop open tools, accessible content, and applied experiences that help people better understand how information circulates and how to use technology in a more conscious and productive way.

items.title: Diagnosis and listening
items.body: Processes to understand how different audiences get informed, what difficulties they face, and what kind of support they need.

items.title: Methodologies for territories and institutions
items.body: Design and implementation of working models for municipalities, schools, organisations, and public services.

items.title: Open resources and tools
items.body: Guides, activities, explanatory materials, participatory experiences, and conversational tools adapted to different audiences and life stages.

items.title: Support for institutional adoption
items.body: Support to teams and organisations to incorporate this agenda from their own roles, capacities, and objectives.

items.title: Evidence for decisions
items.body: Systematising lessons learned and generating useful inputs to improve practices, programmes, and ways of communicating.

partners.title: Who we work with
items: Teams working with older adults, youth, or specific communities

outcomes.title: What we aim to leave behind
outcomes.prologue: Our goal is not simply to run activities. We aim for every organisation we work with to be left with:
items: Better tools to understand how their audiences relate to information
Usable resources adaptable to their reality
Capacities to sustain this work over time
A stronger connection between evidence, communication, and action

approach.title: Our approach to collaboration
paras: We believe that tools work better when they are born from real use, local context, and people’s experience.

That’s why at Precisar we understand collaboration as a process of joint construction. We bring evidence, methodological design, pedagogical experience, and tool development; our partners bring territorial knowledge, community reach, local legitimacy, and implementation capacity. That combination allows us to build responses that are more relevant, clearer, and more sustainable.

why.title: Why work with Precisar
why.prologue: Because we combine four capacities that don’t usually come together:
items: Useful evidence to understand how people get informed and what they need.
Applied methodologies to translate that evidence into concrete intervention.
Accessible resources designed for different audiences and contexts.
Institutional support so capacities stay in place.

cta.title: Let’s talk
cta.body: If your organisation wants to develop tools, methodologies, or experiences to strengthen people’s relationship with information and promote a more conscious, useful, and constructive use of technology, we’d be glad to talk.
